statement: The Gallup Þjóðarpúls survey conducted February–March 2026 (sample size approximately 9,958, participation rate 43.3%) showed 42% of Icelanders support EU membership and 42% oppose it, with approximately 16% undecided. This represents a statistical dead heat, consistent with the trend of gradually rising support from ~35% in 2022 to ~45% in late 2025, now stabilising. A separate question on the upcoming referendum found 52% favour continuing EU accession negotiations versus 48% opposed — a higher figure than membership support, reflecting voters who want negotiations to proceed without necessarily supporting the end result of membership.
caveats: The 42/42 split is within the margin of error. Online panel surveys (which Gallup Þjóðarpúls uses) may under-represent older and rural voters who tend to be more EU-sceptic. The gap between 'support negotiations' (52%) and 'support membership' (42%) is significant — roughly 10% of voters want to negotiate but are not yet committed to joining.

related_entries.statement: Gallup polls conducted in Iceland between 2022 and early 2026 show a gradual shift in public opinion on EU membership. Support for applying for EU membership rose from approximately 30% in 2022 to around 45% by late 2025, while opposition declined from roughly 50% to 40%. The remainder were undecided. The trend accelerated following the Althingi's decision in 2024 to hold a binding referendum.

Icelandic public opinion on EU membership has swung dramatically since the 2009 application. Support peaked at approximately 55% in early 2009, during the financial crisis when EU/euro membership was seen as a stability anchor. By 2013, support had fallen to approximately 25% as the economy recovered and the centre-right government suspended negotiations. Support gradually recovered to ~30% by 2018, ~35% by 2022, and ~45% by late 2025. The 2024 Althingi vote to hold a binding referendum re-energised the debate. Throughout this period, the proportion of undecided voters ranged from 15–25%, representing the decisive swing group.

The Gallup Þjóðarpúls survey on the EU referendum was conducted online from 19 February to 4 March 2026 with a total sample size of 1,898 respondents randomly selected from Gallup's opinion panel. The response rate was 42.7%. The survey asked two main questions: whether respondents support holding a referendum on continuing EU accession negotiations, and how they would likely vote. Key headline findings: 57% support holding the referendum, approximately 30% oppose, and roughly 12% are neutral or undecided. On voting intention if a referendum were held: support for resuming negotiations leads, but when asked about actual EU membership (as opposed to negotiations), the split is closer to 42%–42% with 16% undecided. The gap between support for negotiations and support for membership is a significant methodological distinction in interpreting the results.

citations.report_title: Landbúnaðarstefna ESB henti íslenskum landbúnaði illa

claims.claim_text: Í þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslunni verða tveir valkostir: já til áframhaldandi viðræðna eða nei.

citations.report_title: Þjóðin á báðum áttum um viðræður: 52% gegn 48%

claims.claim_text: Rúmlega 52% landsmanna telja að þau myndu kjósa með því að halda aðildarviðræðum við ESB áfram í þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu.
Tæplega 48% landsmanna telja að þau myndu kjósa með því að hætta aðildarviðræðum við ESB.
Landsmenn skiptast í tvo álíka stóra hópa þegar kemur að því hvernig þeir myndu greiða atkvæði í þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu um framhald aðildarviðræðna.
52 prósent myndu kjósa með áframhaldandi aðildarviðræðum ef þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslan væri í dag.
48 prósent myndu vilja hætta aðildarviðræðum ef þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslan væri í dag.
Lítill munur er á hlutfalli þeirra sem myndu kjósa með og á móti áframhaldandi aðildarviðræðum (52% gegn 48%).
